attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a 3-hour history of sudden onset, progressively worsening severe left calf pain with associated forefoot weakness and numbness. His medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes and osteoarthritis of his knees. His most recent HbA1c was 11 %. His medications include perindopril and metformin. He drinks two glasses of wine a day and has a 30-pack-year smoking history.

On physical examination, his pulse rate is 110/min and irregular, blood pressure 138/90 mmHg, SpO2 96 % on room air and respiratory rate 18/min. Abdominal examination is unremarkable. On peripheral vascular examination, the left lower limb appears ischaemic. There is left calf tenderness, reduced motor function of the left forefoot and toes with associated reduction in sensation. The capillary refill time in the left foot is prolonged at six seconds. No tissue loss is noted. Bilateral femoral pulses are palpable. There is a right popliteal and posterior tibial pulse however no other pulses are felt in left leg.

No audible arterial doppler signal is appreciated in the left foot. A CT angiogram (CTA) of the aorta and lower limbs is performed, showing an embolus in the distal left superficial femoral artery and popliteal artery that appears to have minimal calcification. There is delayed contrast enhancement of the left tibial arteries. The right leg has diffusely calcified but patent arteries.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to anticoagulation, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is suffering from a Rutherford grade 2B acute limb ischaemia (ALI). The Rutherford classification is a useful determinant whether a limb is viable or threatened and the escalation for an immediate revascularisation.

It is recommended that patients with a grade 2b (threatened limb, with neurological weakness +/- sensory changes) to be immediately transferred to a hospital with both vascular and endovascular facilities for a hybrid approach. These cases will need more activation than anticoagulation alone. The most appropriate intervention in these circumstances is thrombo-embolectomy +/- thrombolysisis. This approach is recommended as a gold standard management of this ischaemic limb unless there are contraindications to surgery or thrombolysis. It is likely that this is a cardio-embolus given the atrial fibrillation, especially in the absence of significant atherosclerotic disease.

Atherectomy and angioplasty +/- stenting would be the highly considered surgical intervention if the aetiology of the ALI was a haemodynamically significant stenosis secondary to an atherosclerotic plaque resulting in-situ thrombosis of the vessel.

A bypass surgery is often reserved as an option when all other revascularisation options have failed. Native great saphenous vein (reversed or non-reversed) or prosthetic graft (dacron or polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)) are used as conduits for the bypass.

Major limb amputation is reserved for Rutherford's 3 ischaemic limbs (irreversible ischaemia) that are non salvageable.
learningPoints: Rutherford classification for ALI is a good marker of severity of disease and helps with directing patient management: conservative, open surgical +/- endovascular or amputation.
references: Björck M, Earnshaw JJ, Acosta S, Bastos Gonçalves F, Cochennec F, Debus ES, et al. Editor's Choice - European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) 2020 Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Acute Limb Ischaemia. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg. 2020;59(2):173-218. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31899099/

Olinic DM, Stanek A, Tătaru DA, Homorodean C, Olinic M. Acute Limb Ischemia: An Update on Diagnosis and Management. J Clin Med. 2019;8(8):1215. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6723825/
